In this lesson, we'll go over the process for financial due diligence. This step typically happens after we finalized a letter of intent and we're working towards a final agreement. This is your opportunity to really dig into the books and confirm the economic situation of the business. You'll look for consistencies among accounts, assets, and liabilities. You'll also look at historical trends, projections, and tax risks. When done right, this can be one of the most tedious and time-consuming parts of the business buying process. It's not to be skipped or short-changed. There are dozens of ways that companies can make their financial statements look better than they actually are. So I want to start this lesson by asking you to get yourself into the proper mindset for this particular process. You should approach the financial due diligence process like a trained detective. No number should be accepted at face value. No question. Or inquiry is too small. Never forget, the numbers aren't always the numbers. From my experience, anytime a seller is selling their business, they shape the numbers you see to create the value they want. It's critically important to verify the information that they provide to you and ensure that the bad isn't understated and the good isn't overstated. Now we will cover a ton of information in this lesson, but don't worry. I've broken it down into easy to follow checklists. And remember, you should not be doing this alone. A qualified and trusted accountant with experience and acquisitions should be doing most of this heavy lifting here. You simply need to act as a quarterback in the process and be critically involved. Ensure you go through this full checklist with your accountant and hold them to finding the answers to every question that might arise. After all, you do not want to get stuck in a situation where you're surprised by some undiscovered liability. This process is also an amazing opportunity for you to get your mind fully wrapped around the business economics, which will help you with future planning and decision making as a new owner. And now with that in mind, let's get started. There are eight steps to completing financial due diligence. Step number one is the counting standards. Step number two is revenue analysis. Step number three is cost and pricing analysis. Step four is balance sheet review. Step five is cash flow analysis and planning. Step six is past and future projections. Step seven is debts and contracts. And finally, step eight is tax risks. As I go through each of these items, I will give you additional checklists and questions to consider depending on the type of business industry you're buying. So let's start off by discussing the various types of accounting standards. An accounting standard is a set of practices and policies used to systematize bookkeeping. Publicly traded companies are held to the most rigorous accounting standards. In the United States, the SEC requires these companies to file GAAP-compliant financial statements. GAAP stands for Generally Accepted Accounting Principles. When a company's statements are audited, they are often also held to those same standards. This gives people reviewing the statements a high level of assurance in these statements. However, you'll be performing due diligence on privately owned companies and they probably will not be keeping their books up to this standard. It's frankly unlikely they're keeping their books to any standard. So this is why you need to pay particular attention to the financials. You simply need to take note of how consistent and detailed the statements are that were given to you from the seller. Ask your accountant to give you on a scale of 1 to 10, 1, very poor 10, unbelievably accurate, how well the financials that you've been given are kept track of. Also make sure you know if they're using cash based accounting standards or if they're using a cruel. This is a pretty big red flag. It's very easy to manipulate financials with cash accounting by the way. It's very easy to book revenue in one year and push costs into another year. This is not a deal breaker, but it certainly will help guide you in the coming sections of this due diligence process. Way back in lesson module two, we discussed what financial statements you'll need to request and how to request them. You should request financial statements for a minimum of the past three years, plus an interim income statement for the current fiscal year that you're in when you made the deal. Ideally, their accountant includes notes to the far right of each financial statement. And you'll also want to ensure you get a general ledger for each year. You'll also want a copy of the balance sheets for the same time period, and you'll need cash flow statements for the last six months for certain. Now let's get to work on actually digging into the financials. We'll start by doing a revenue analysis. The first thing that we want to do is dig deep into the various sources of revenue for the business. You want to itemize all of your major revenue streams and find out exactly where they're coming from. The biggest thing you're looking for here is clients or revenue sources that dominate a majority of the revenue coming into the business. If one or two sources are making up more than 40% of the business's revenue, you want to make sure you understand who those clients are if they have a contract and what the probability of them leaving the organization might be. The business is highly dependent upon these customers. The business is fragile if those customers leave and it could be devastating for a new buyer. You want to make sure you understand who they are, what kind of agreements they have, and how long those agreements are for. This might, however, not be an issue if part of your plan is to bolt this business to an existing business that will diversify your customer base. But if you're buying this business, you must understand the longevity of that relationship and the likelihood that they'll continue to work with you. Next, you want to break down your revenue into products and services or SKUs. What are you selling and how much revenue is being generated from each item? You also want to look at the customer and client turnover and repurchasing behaviors. You'll want to look at churn rate, how often that customers come and go so you can get an idea of the serviceability of the business to those customers. You'll want to know how frequently new customers are being brought into the business and at what rate they repurchase and at what rate customers are being lost. That's called churn rate. So, I'll give you an example. I purchased a business that had a very diversified clientele. The problem was that clientele was used to working with the business on a technology that was being sun-setted. So all of a sudden, I'm buying a business that has historical clients, a lot of them, doing a lot of business, but the business sun-setted its technology because its license has expired. So, now all of a sudden, when I reach out to those businesses, I found that they were all moving to a different technology that had a current license. I could have bought a business that literally went to zero revenue if I wasn't paying attention to the churn of the customers and noticed an uptick in the last four months before the business sold. The owner was trying to exit the business before it went to zero. So paying attention to details like this are critically important to the success of an acquisition. A few more items on the checklist. And remember, you can find this full list in the resources section of this module. Identify key performance indicators that are driving the business. You need to know what builds the pipeline of new customers in revenue. How well is this pipeline converting potential customers or clients into paying customers and clients? Also, what new products and services are in the pipeline? When was the last new product or service introduced and what is the lifetime value of that product or service? Track product sales by volume over time. Are the units sold per month decreasing every month? It's really important to get a clear picture of where revenue is coming from and any weaknesses or opportunities within the business. Revenue drives the business forward, but costs can slow the business down. This is why the next thing we need to dig into is the costs and pricing analysis. With the help of your accountant, you want to identify the cost structure of the business. There are fixed costs and variable costs. Fixed costs are costs that the company has to pay irrespective of revenue. So things like salaries, utilities, rent, leases, truck payments, things of this nature. Those would be considered fixed costs. A variable cost is an expense that changes in proportion to how much the company produces or sells, like a marketing expense. That could be a variable cost. You could choose to spend more or less. This would include marketing expenses, cost of goods, commission to salespeople, etc. The ratio of these fixed versus variable costs is what makes up the company's cost structure. Then this cost structure is used to fix the prices you charge customers and clients. That is if the company is using a cost-based pricing model. The cost structure also gives you a very clear picture of exactly what it costs to run the business and produce revenue. Cost-driven business models minimize costs wherever possible, often through a low-price value proposition maximizing automation and extensive outsourcing. Whereas value-driven companies focus a premium value proposition often with a high degree of personalized services. Cost-driven businesses are often lower in variable costs, but potentially also have lower prices for their services. Value-driven businesses typically have higher variable costs and warrant higher prices. Designer clothing, luxury hotels, assets, fancy cars, things of this nature, those would drive a higher price, a higher cost. It's important for you to understand the fixed and variable cost in the business, how much it costs to run the business, and understand current pricing models in relation to costs. Next, we will take a careful look at a balance sheet. As a reminder, a company's balance sheet, also known as a statement of financial position, reveals the firm's assets, liabilities, and owner's equity. One of the key things we're looking for here is capital expenditures. Capital expenditures are used by companies to acquire, upgrade, and maintain physical assets such as property, plants, buildings, technology, or equipment. We're looking for any place that people might be manipulating their company to make it look more profitable in the year of the sale. One easy way to do this is push off capital expenditures. If an owner has been light on capital expenditures for several years, in anticipation of wanting to sell the business, you might walk into a business that immediately needs a significant amount of maintenance and upgrades. These costs could obviously lower your profit margins after you took over the business. You'll want to not only see what the business has spent in capital expenditures, but also do some research on what you should expect to spend when you become the business owner. You also want to ask for a list of all major equipment and the date of purchase and or maintenance and any contracts on that equipment. The bottom line is that you need to know what your exposure is on capital expenditures should you take control of the business. You'll also want to try to get an accurate count of all fixed assets. If the assets of the business are bolted to the ground or in one place like a warehouse, it's very easy to count them. But if the asset is mobile like trucks, equipment used on job sites, etc., it's not easy to get them all in one place. It's very important to have an accurate count as soon as possible. You'll need a very clear picture of all assets you should be acquiring with the business. You'll also want to request an age receivables report. This is a breakdown of all receivables due from customers broken down by the length of time the invoice has been outstanding. You likely already have received an accounts receivable report earlier in the process. It's important, however, to request this again. The final funding is going to be based on the most up-to-date receivables. If you're using receivables in the process of securing financing, a lender will not include any invoice over 90 to 120 days outstanding. Having that age receivables report is very important. The goal is to track down bad debt. That is debt that cannot be collected. Some people see any bad debt as a red flag. However, this is not necessarily true. You don't want to have too much bad debt. Sure, that's a sign that you have a very questionable customer base. However, if you have zero bad debt, then you're either potentially not bringing in very many new customers or you're turning away customers due to a strict credit requirement or something. Zero bad debt is a sign of a weak influx of new customers. You also want to review inventory stock and identify any unusable goods. An easy way to do this is to identify any products or SKUs that do not turn very frequently. This is a sign that they do not sell very well. These unsellable goods might be left on the balance sheet as an asset, but the truth is they might be an unusable asset. Next, you should evaluate unearned revenue. This will show up on the balance sheet under liabilities. These might be things like customer deposits on work that's not been completed yet. These are things that you're going to have to finish when you buy the business. There is obviously a cost associated with doing that work. You need to ensure that you're accounting for this cost and deducting it from the business's assets. If the company has $100,000 in cash, but a cost of $20,000 in undelivered work, then the company's asset should be reduced by $20,000. You'll need to take all of this into account and adjust your valuations or offer accordingly. Again, you don't want any surprises when you take over the business. This entire due diligence section is about rolling up your sleeves and really understanding the business from every angle. With that being said, let's move on to the looking at the cash flow statement and cash planning. Right away, you should identify how much cash is on hand and how many months you could run the business with that cash. Do you have enough cash to run the business should there be a downturn? Is the business prepared for any price fluctuations and cost for goods or materials? An example of that is recently in the last year. If you bought a contracting company, they had a bunch of open jobs and had taken deposits. You're now delivering the work required based on the contract for a cost structure that's 38% on average higher than it was a year ago because the goods, the stuff you're buying to do, the materials, the labor, it cost 38% more. Imagine buying a business and having that business only generate 20% in profit and now you're 20% backwards because of commitments that were made last year. You should identify if the business is susceptible to currency or commodity gains or losses. You should also find out if there's open contracts, they've taken those deposits and what the costing to finish those contracts is going to be for future projects. The same is also true for things like commodities that are purchased in a one time period and sold or used in another. This brings us back to our contractor example. One of our clients bought a whole bunch of air conditioning units at $300 a unit. But today, a year later, they're worth $1,800 a unit because people can't get those units. So every job they do today is going to be padded at $1,500 in more profit today than it actually is. So understanding what's going on with the costing side of the business is critically important because you can be buying a business with assumptions on certain margins that are just not going to be true today. Finally, it's also our job as a business owner to know the yearly life cycle of cash in our business. We need to identify the low points and plan for it. In addition, identify extraneous costs that we can strip out and possibly strengthen the cash flow of the business. Next, we'd like to talk about past and present business projections. You'll want to request a forecast or projection model from the business owner. Now, I'll be honest with you, 99.9% of all business owners aren't going to have anything like this, but you should request it because that tells you how in tune they are with their own business. But there are two types of forecasting models. Top down and bottom up. Top down forecasting takes a bird's eye view of the entire market and projects the business's market share. It uses this to estimate future revenue opportunities. I'm admittedly oversimplifying this, but the idea is that it's a very subjective approach to forecasting with many more assumptions. A bottom up approach, however, consists of breaking a business apart into underlying key components that ultimately drive its revenue generation and growth. This is a much more accurate and reliable model. Regardless of the model type, you're looking for bad logic or bad assumptions within the projections. By this point, you hopefully have a good grasp on what really drives the business. You'll need to apply this knowledge to ensuring these drivers are actually represented in the projections the business owner provides you. You also should request past projections the company did to help them manage the business in previous years. This will give you a lot of foresight if they ever hit their goals on what kind of planning they were doing. Are these people any good at forecasting? That's the question you should ask yourself. If they're not doing any forecasting, the answer is absolutely no. You can easily tell if they are by comparing their previous projections to the actual past year's financial statements. Put the two together and see how close or how far they missed. Next, on your due diligence checklist, you want to take a very close look at existing debts and contracts. To review the company's existing liabilities or debt, you'll want to jump back into the balance sheet. You'll want to take a close look at all the liabilities simply because you should know what debt you're taking on as a new business owner. But it is especially important if you're trying to leverage the assets for financing the funding. You'll need to determine how many of these assets are already being used to secure debt. You don't want to have too much existing debt on the company if you're trying to leverage it in an LBO strategy. You're looking for a low debt to equity ratio. Furthermore, you need to review all loan covenants on existing loans very closely. These can be ticking time bombs for you. Ensure you know all existing liabilities that you'll take care of as the owner and ensure you understand any pressure that this might put on you in the business. For example, if there's a loan on the business and it's non-transferable, the covenants say that cannot be assigned. You pay the owner and the owner goes away and then all of a sudden the bank has a lien on your assets, you're going to find yourself in trouble. The same is true for contracts. You need to review a slew of contracts with any given business before you acquire it. Every single contract should be reviewed to ensure it's a contract you can live with. You want to get your hands on the most important contracts and review them carefully. There can be any number of pitfalls hidden in them and you'll need to know and be prepared for those pitfalls. I'll give you another example. I had a client that bought a business that was in a prime location and the business had been there for years, years and years and years and that value of the location became huge. The client acquired that business. The landlord had a non-transferable position in the lease. Then they booted the person out of that location that that business had been there for 20 years because the land was worth more than the lease was paying. Now there's nothing the buyer could do. They had to move out because they did not read the lease. They did not understand it was non-transferable and they got booted out. It's things like this that could really devastate or disrupt a business. You'll want to request contracts for customers that represent a significant stream of revenue as well along with expiration dates. Read every contract. It can make you or break you. If you have a large customer and they have a breakup provision, if there's a transfer of the business, you're going to want to know that, address it and make it part of the deal that they sign a new contract or you're going to lower the purchase price. Frankly, you should first ask what percent of the customers are under contract. Most likely none of them. But depending on the kind of business, they may be. Often business owners get very lazy with contracts and might have paying customers with no contracts. In fact, I have another example. Somebody bought the stock of a business and a customer found out they had been paying on an expired contract and demanded the business repay those payments because the business owner bought the stock, they inherited the liabilities. Now good luck going after the seller to get that money back. So you need to pay attention to every single contract or you will find yourself potentially in trouble. Contracts that have expired or are not renewed in the last six months need to be itemized out in your closing documents and you need to put a value around them and the business owner needs to get new contracts with them or you deduct the purchase price. That's your responsibility to know what those contracts say and to tie them to the purchase of the business. It's very important to understand the why behind inactive clients lost contracts and contracts facing expiration. You don't want to buy a business that's going to be naturally dying because customers are leaving and you don't know that. I would call every customer that stopped buying from that business within the last six months and I would ask them why they're no longer working with that business. You also want to request all material contracts. What agreements do they have with suppliers for materials? You know, I also have another story here. I had a customer that prepaid, prepaid for volume and then when the buyer came in they assumed that was the price for the product. But because they bought in mass volume on a one time deal they were able to get the product so low that every product purchases after that for the new buyer were at a much higher price. Pay attention with what kind of deals these businesses have with their product or material suppliers. When they do expire or come up for renegotiation you need to understand do you have leverage in that conversation and what are your alternatives to get better pricing if you do. Some businesses on the other side have contracts where they're paying a higher price and you find out you can get it cheaper. So understanding the duration of the contracts and what your responsibilities are is also something you need to know about. Another story. I had a customer that got a pre-bate. So if you don't know what a pre-bate is it was a pre-payment for a rebate. So all of a sudden all this money was given to the business. It shows its profitability with a lower cost of goods and then all that money went away the next year because it was a one time deal. You need to look at any anomalies in the business and track them to the suppliers to understand what was going on. One final item on our financial due diligence checklist is tax risks. You will want to request the past five years of tax returns for review. You should also ask to review any previous tax audits, reviews or any other correspondence with the business that's been had with the IRS in the past five years. The biggest thing that you're trying to determine is if there's any money owed to the IRS, especially if you're going to do a stock purchase, you will end up owing that money. You also need a good accountant to look them over to ensure that there are no filing vulnerabilities that might come back to haunt you in future audits. One easy mistake a lot of businesses make is to treat contractors as employees. The IRS does not look fondly on this and could audit and ask for back taxes from the business. This is exactly why I mentioned earlier I don't really like stock purchases because you inherit any past risks associated with buying that business. You want to ensure you are aware of all tax vulnerabilities before taking over the company and you want to have provisions in your agreements that ensure if anything comes out of the woodwork that the seller is paying for it. Also pay close attention to the allocation of purchase dollars, whether that be depreciated or accelerated against income. A skilled tax advisor can assist you in determining which hard asset you can accelerate depreciation or capitalization on. A tax advisor is essential to optimizing and tax advantaging the deal. And all of this due diligence will pay off not only to avoid getting yourself in a hard situation but also identify a number of opportunities you can exploit when you take over the business. And this is pretty much it for the financial due diligence. Take this very serious, I promise you. It's one of the most important things you'll ever do in your professional career. I will state it again, you should not do this alone. You need a trained accountant that is familiar with acquisitions to assist you. Your job is to have your checklist of questions and make sure you understand all the answers before moving forward with a deal. You'll learn even more about due diligence in the rest of this module.
